refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry and features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent DP selection and IM packaging.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature database) and can be queried by downstream planning and execution functions to retrieve spatial attributes (position, geometry, classification) for an agreed set of site features.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and restricted areas in the spatial model.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and provide access to the worksite spatial model for planning and execution functions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured geometric model (point clouds, meshes, or voxel grids) with semantic labels and metadata; expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign each zone a feature inventory (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; represent as a graph of zone nodes with edge constraints; persist in a queryable schema.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a rule-based symbolic representation (e.g., logic rules or ontology) that encodes spatial relationships, safety constraints, and operational policies; derive a queryable model on demand by evaluating rules against sensor observations.

DP4: Establish a real-time streaming pipeline that continuously ingests sensor data, applies feature extraction and classification, and maintains an incrementally updated spatial model; expose model state via subscription/polling interface for planning/execution.

DP5: Delegate feature capture to a hybrid approach: use pre-defined templates (e.g., site layout blueprints, CAD models) as a baseline; augment with live sensor observations to detect deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features; merge into a composite model.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Hybrid baseline-augmented model. Establish a composite spatial representation by anchoring to pre-defined worksite templates (blueprints, CAD, or prior maps) and augmenting with live multi-modal sensor observations to detect deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features. Fuse baseline and live data into a unified, queryable geometric and semantic model. Expose model state and feature queries via a standardized interface for planning and execution consumers. Maintain model freshness and consistency through incremental updates and conflict resolution policies aligned with safety and operational constraints.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor modality selection and feature extraction logic—mitigated by abstracting sensor input as a generic observation stream and keeping extraction logic modular.

Dependency on baseline template availability and accuracy—mitigated by graceful degradation: if baseline unavailable, fall back to DP1 (pure sensor fusion); if baseline inaccurate, detection of deviations triggers re-calibration.
Coupling between model update frequency and planning/execution cycle time—mitigated by decoupling via asynchronous query interface; consumers poll or subscribe at their own cadence.
Potential coupling to specific safety/operational policy encoding—mitigated by externalizing policies as queryable constraints rather than hard-coding into model structure.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves loose coupling by separating concerns: baseline provision (external), live sensing (pluggable), fusion logic (centralized), and policy enforcement (declarative). The hybrid approach reduces brittleness compared to pure sensor fusion (DP1) or pure symbolic reasoning (DP3), and supports incremental model refinement without full re-acquisition.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite baseline (blueprint, CAD, or prior map) is available and reasonably accurate at mission start. RISK: If baseline is stale, missing, or significantly inaccurate, fusion may produce misleading composite model. MITIGATION: Implement baseline validation checks; flag high-confidence deviations; support manual override.

ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensor suite (vision, range, inertial) is available and calibrated. RISK: Sensor failures, miscalibration, or occlusions degrade feature detection. MITIGATION: Implement sensor health monitoring; use redundancy where safety-critical; graceful degradation to available modalities.

ASSUMPTION: Feature extraction and semantic labeling can be automated or semi-automated. RISK: Misclassification of hazards or features leads to unsafe planning. MITIGATION: Conservative labeling (over-report hazards); human-in-the-loop validation for high-risk features; traceability of labeling confidence.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formalized and queryable. RISK: Policies are ambiguous, incomplete, or conflict with model representation. MITIGATION: Explicit policy schema; validation against model; escalation for unresolvable conflicts.

ASSUMPTION: Planning and execution functions can tolerate model latency and eventual consistency. RISK: Stale model leads to unsafe or inefficient decisions. MITIGATION: Define acceptable model age; implement update prioritization; alert consumers to model freshness.

ASSUMPTION: Composite model can be efficiently queried and updated in real-time. RISK: Model complexity or update frequency exceeds computational budget. MITIGATION: Hierarchical model representation; lazy evaluation; caching; spatial indexing.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP5 to explicitly separate three sub-concerns: (1) Baseline provisioning and validation (DP5a), (2) Live feature detection and anomaly flagging (DP5b), and (3) Fusion, conflict resolution, and query interface (DP5c). This decomposition clarifies ownership boundaries (IM layer) and allows independent evolution of baseline sources, sensor pipelines, and model consumers. Additionally, introduce a formal model schema (e.g., GeoJSON + semantic tags + policy constraints) to ensure interoperability and auditability. Define acceptance criteria for model freshness, feature detection recall/precision, and policy compliance.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial representation engine that ingests baseline worksite templates (blueprints, CAD, prior maps) and fuses them with live multi-modal sensor observations. Maintains a unified, queryable geometric and semantic model of the worksite state, detects deviations and dynamic hazards, resolves conflicts between baseline and live data, and exposes model state and feature queries to planning and execution consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives live multi-modal sensor observations (LiDAR, camera, thermal, etc.) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to augment baseline model.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Exposes queryable model state and feature queries (geometric and semantic) to planning and execution consumers for mission planning and real-time decision-making.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Publishes detected deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features that violate safety constraints to Safety Services for hazard mitigation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates baseline template data (blueprints, CAD, prior maps) ingestion and model synchronization across distributed nodes if multi-agent or cloud-edge architecture is present.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE (Next-Generation Hazard Estimation) is a spatial reasoning and environmental modeling platform. The hybrid baseline-augmented model, sensor fusion, conflict resolution, and queryable geometric/semantic representation are core to NGHE's mission. Baseline template ingestion, live sensor augmentation, deviation detection, and model freshness are all native to this module. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion (from Vehicle/Teleoperation) and safety alert publication (to Safety Services) cross boundaries.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a platform for actuation, locomotion, and sensor payload hosting. The spatial model, fusion logic, baseline template management, and conflict resolution are not vehicle-specific concerns. Placing this DP in Vehicle would scatter model logic across the vehicle control layer, creating tight coupling between vehicle dynamics and environmental reasoning. High diffusion: model queries would need to propagate back to planning systems, and baseline templates would need to be distributed to every vehicle instance.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop control interface for remote operation. While it may consume model queries for visualization, it is not responsible for maintaining the authoritative spatial model, fusing baseline and live data, or managing conflict resolution. Placing this DP in Teleoperation would conflate operator interface concerns with core environmental reasoning. High diffusion: the model would need to be replicated or synchronized across multiple teleoperation stations, and planning systems would lack direct access to the model.

Weak fit. Safety Services is responsible for hazard detection, risk assessment, and safety constraint enforcement. While it consumes hazard alerts from the spatial model, it should not own the model itself. Placing this DP in Safety Services would conflate environmental representation with safety policy enforcement. High diffusion: the baseline template ingestion, sensor fusion, and geometric/semantic queries are not safety-specific; they are foundational to all planning and execution. Safety Services should be a consumer of model state, not its owner.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of the hybrid baseline-augmented spatial model. NGHE's core mission is to provide next-generation hazard estimation and environmental reasoning. The DP—which requires baseline template management, multi-modal sensor fusion, conflict resolution, and queryable geometric/semantic representation—is a foundational capability that NGHE must provide to all downstream consumers (planning, execution, safety, teleoperation). Containment is strong because all core logic (fusion, conflict resolution, model freshness) resides within NGHE, and external interfaces are well-defined and minimal (telemetry in, tasking and safety alerts out).
containment_risks: Risk: If baseline templates (blueprints, CAD, prior maps) are managed by an external system (e.g., a mission planning or asset management system), NGHE must have a clear ingestion interface and versioning strategy to avoid stale or inconsistent baselines.

Risk: Multi-modal sensor fusion is computationally intensive. If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems generate high-frequency telemetry, NGHE must implement efficient buffering, decimation, or edge-processing strategies to avoid bottlenecks.

Risk: Conflict resolution policies (baseline vs. live data) must be aligned with safety and operational constraints. If these policies are defined or updated by Safety Services or mission planning, NGHE must expose a configuration interface to avoid hardcoding policies.

Risk: Model freshness and consistency are critical for safety. If NGHE operates in a distributed or multi-agent environment, synchronization and consensus mechanisms must be robust to network delays and failures.

Risk: Queryable model interface (Tasking) must be well-specified and versioned to avoid tight coupling with planning and execution consumers. Changes to model schema or query semantics could break downstream systems.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR requirement ('Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model') is directly verifiable through acceptance criteria: (1) worksite model/map generation and availability to planning/execution functions, and (2) coverage of agreed site features. NGHE Solution's queryable geometric/semantic interface (Tasking) provides the mechanism for verification. Model freshness and consistency can be monitored through telemetry ingestion rates, conflict resolution audit logs, and query response latencies.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. NGHE Solution is the single authoritative owner of the spatial model. Allocation boundaries are well-defined: NGHE ingests baseline templates (Network Link in) and live telemetry (Telemetry in), maintains the unified model internally, and exposes queries (Tasking out) and safety alerts (SafetyAlert out). No ambiguity regarding which module owns model state, fusion logic, or conflict resolution.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (Hybrid baseline-augmented model) directly addresses the FR. The DP specifies the solution approach: (1) anchor to pre-defined worksite templates, (2) augment with live multi-modal sensor observations, (3) detect deviations and dynamic hazards, (4) fuse into unified queryable model, (5) maintain freshness and consistency. This decomposition is complete and logically sound. The DP is neither over-constrained (it does not prescribe specific algorithms or technologies) nor under-constrained (it defines clear responsibilities: baseline ingestion, sensor fusion, conflict resolution, query interface, model freshness). Alignment with acceptance criteria is strong: the model is machine-readable (geometric/semantic), available to planning/execution (Tasking interface), and covers agreed site features (queryable by planning consumers).

diffusion_risks: Baseline template versioning and staleness: If baseline templates (blueprints, CAD, prior maps) are sourced from an external mission planning or asset management system, NGHE must implement a clear ingestion protocol, version tracking, and staleness detection. Risk of diffusion: baseline management logic could leak into Network Link interface or require bidirectional synchronization, complicating ownership.

High-frequency telemetry buffering and decimation: Multi-modal sensor fusion is computationally intensive. If Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystems generate telemetry faster than NGHE can process, buffering and decimation strategies must be implemented within NGHE. Risk of diffusion: if edge-processing or filtering is pushed to Vehicle or Teleoperation to reduce load, model logic becomes scattered across modules, violating single responsibility.

Conflict resolution policy configuration: Conflict resolution policies (baseline vs. live data) must be aligned with safety and operational constraints. If these policies are defined externally (e.g., by Safety Services or mission planning), NGHE must expose a configuration interface. Risk of diffusion: if policies are hardcoded or tightly coupled to Safety Services, policy updates require NGHE code changes, creating tight coupling.

Distributed synchronization and consensus: If NGHE operates in a multi-agent or cloud-edge architecture, model synchronization across nodes must be robust. Risk of diffusion: if each agent maintains a local replica of the model, conflict resolution and consistency mechanisms could be scattered across multiple instances, creating eventual consistency risks and safety vulnerabilities.

Queryable model interface schema evolution: The Tasking interface (model state and feature queries) must be well-versioned. Risk of diffusion: if planning and execution consumers tightly couple to model schema, schema changes in NGHE could break downstream systems. Requires clear API versioning and deprecation policies.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.95 score justified). NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the hybrid baseline-augmented spatial model. All core logic—baseline template ingestion, multi-modal sensor fusion, conflict resolution, geometric/semantic representation, model freshness—resides within NGHE. External interfaces are minimal and well-defined: Telemetry (in), Tasking (out), SafetyAlert (out), Network Link (inout). No significant diffusion to Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services. However, containment risks arise at the boundaries: (1) baseline template sourcing and versioning must be managed with clear ownership of the ingestion protocol, (2) telemetry ingestion rates and buffering strategies must be tuned to avoid bottlenecks, (3) conflict resolution policies must be configurable without requiring code changes, (4) distributed synchronization (if applicable) must be robust and well-specified, (5) Tasking interface schema must be versioned and stable. These are integration and operational concerns, not fundamental containment issues.

recommended_revisions: Establish a baseline template ingestion and versioning protocol: Define a clear interface for baseline template sourcing (blueprints, CAD, prior maps), including version tracking, staleness detection, and rollback mechanisms. Specify ownership of template management (e.g., mission planning system provides templates, NGHE ingests and validates). Document the protocol in the Network Link interface specification.

Specify telemetry ingestion SLAs and buffering strategy: Define maximum telemetry ingestion rates, buffering capacity, and decimation/filtering policies within NGHE. Establish SLAs for telemetry latency and model update frequency. Document these in the Telemetry interface specification to avoid ad-hoc buffering logic in Vehicle or Teleoperation.

Expose conflict resolution policy configuration interface: Create a configuration mechanism within NGHE to allow external systems (Safety Services, mission planning) to define or update conflict resolution policies without code changes. Document policy schema, update semantics, and safety constraints. This prevents hardcoding and reduces coupling.

Define distributed synchronization and consensus strategy (if multi-agent): If NGHE operates across multiple agents or cloud-edge nodes, specify synchronization protocol, conflict resolution for concurrent updates, and eventual consistency guarantees. Document in Network Link interface. Ensure safety-critical model state (e.g., detected hazards) is synchronized with strong consistency guarantees.

Establish Tasking interface versioning and stability policy: Define API versioning strategy for model state and feature queries. Specify deprecation policies, backward compatibility guarantees, and schema evolution rules. Document in Tasking interface specification to prevent tight coupling with planning and execution consumers.

Clarify model freshness and consistency metrics: Define quantitative metrics for model freshness (e.g., maximum age of baseline template, maximum latency of live sensor observations) and consistency (e.g., conflict resolution success rate, model query response time). Establish monitoring and alerting to detect staleness or consistency violations.

Document safety-critical model state handling: Specify which model elements are safety-critical (e.g., detected hazards, deviations from baseline) and require immediate propagation to Safety Services. Define SafetyAlert interface semantics, latency requirements, and failure modes.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is clear and verifiable, the DP is logically sound and complete, and NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner with excellent containment (0.95 score). However, the DP-IM gate identifies five material diffusion risks at integration boundaries that require explicit sub-level decomposition to ensure safe, maintainable realization. Given high safety criticality, these risks must be addressed through next-level functional decomposition rather than deferred to implementation. Specifically: (1) baseline template ingestion and versioning, (2) telemetry buffering and decimation, (3) conflict resolution policy configuration, (4) distributed synchronization (if applicable), and (5) Tasking interface schema evolution are all functional concerns that must be explicitly designed and allocated. Proceeding to sub-FR decomposition will make these concerns visible, allocatable, and verifiable.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Ingest, validate, and version baseline worksite templates (blueprints, CAD, prior maps) from external mission planning or asset management systems, with staleness detection and rollback capability.
FR5.2: Buffer, filter, and decimate multi-modal telemetry streams from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to match NGHE processing capacity while maintaining model freshness SLAs.
FR5.3: Detect and resolve conflicts between baseline template features and live sensor observations using configurable policies aligned with safety and operational constraints.
FR5.4: Maintain queryable geometric and semantic representation of worksite features with versioned schema, backward compatibility, and stable API for planning and execution consumers.

FR5.5: Synchronize spatial model state across distributed agents or cloud-edge nodes (if applicable) with strong consistency guarantees for safety-critical hazard detections and eventual consistency for non-critical features.
FR5.6: Monitor and report model freshness, consistency, and conflict resolution metrics to enable detection of staleness, synchronization failures, or safety violations.

assumptions: Baseline templates are sourced from an external mission planning or asset management system and must be ingested via a well-defined protocol.
Multi-modal telemetry is generated at rates that may exceed NGHE processing capacity, requiring buffering and decimation strategies.
Conflict resolution policies are operationally sensitive and must be configurable without code changes to NGHE.
NGHE may operate in a distributed architecture (multi-agent or cloud-edge) requiring synchronization and consensus mechanisms.
Planning and execution consumers depend on stable, versioned Tasking interface schema; schema evolution must be managed carefully.
Safety-critical model state (detected hazards, deviations from baseline) must propagate to Safety Services with low latency and high reliability.
Model freshness and consistency are quantifiable and monitorable; violations must trigger alerts.
open_risks: Baseline template staleness: If templates are not regularly updated or if staleness is not detected, NGHE may operate with outdated baseline, leading to false conflict detections or missed hazards.
Telemetry bottleneck: If telemetry ingestion rates exceed NGHE capacity and buffering/decimation is not properly tuned, model updates may lag, violating freshness SLAs and delaying hazard detection.

Conflict resolution policy coupling: If policies are hardcoded or tightly coupled to Safety Services, policy updates require NGHE code changes, increasing deployment risk and reducing operational agility.

Distributed synchronization failures: If NGHE operates across multiple agents and synchronization is weak or eventual-consistent for safety-critical state, conflicting hazard detections could lead to safety violations.
Tasking interface schema brittleness: If planning and execution consumers tightly couple to model schema, schema changes in NGHE could break downstream systems, requiring coordinated deployments.
Model freshness metric ambiguity: If freshness metrics are not clearly defined (e.g., maximum age of baseline, maximum latency of sensor observations), monitoring and alerting will be ineffective.
Safety-critical state propagation latency: If SafetyAlert interface does not guarantee low-latency propagation of detected hazards, safety response may be delayed.
evidence_needed: Baseline template ingestion protocol specification: Define source systems, versioning scheme, staleness detection logic, and rollback procedures. Validate with mission planning and asset management stakeholders.

Telemetry ingestion SLA specification: Measure typical telemetry rates from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. Define maximum buffering capacity, decimation policies, and model update frequency. Validate with planning and execution consumers.

Conflict resolution policy schema and configuration interface: Define policy language, update semantics, and safety constraints. Prototype configuration mechanism and validate with Safety Services and mission planning.

Distributed synchronization protocol (if applicable): Specify synchronization algorithm, conflict resolution for concurrent updates, and consistency guarantees. Validate with multi-agent or cloud-edge architecture stakeholders.
Tasking interface versioning and stability policy: Define API versioning strategy, deprecation policies, and backward compatibility guarantees. Validate with planning and execution consumers.

Model freshness and consistency metrics: Define quantitative metrics (e.g., maximum baseline age, maximum sensor latency, conflict resolution success rate). Establish monitoring and alerting mechanisms. Validate with Safety Services.

SafetyAlert interface latency and reliability requirements: Measure typical hazard detection latency and define acceptable propagation latency to Safety Services. Validate with safety-critical operations stakeholders.
